retrieved_docs.title: Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The first CAR-T cell treatment approved by the United States Food and Drug Administration(FDA) in August 2017 was tisagenlecleucel. It is indicated in patients up to 25 years of age with B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia that is refractory or in the second or later relapse. It also has indications in adult patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma after 2 or more lines of systemic therapy, including diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) not otherwise specified, high-grade B-cell lymphoma and DLBCL arising from follicular lymphoma. The second US FDA-approved CAR-T cell treatment, in October 2017, is axicabtagene ciloleucel, which is indicated for the treatment of adult patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma after 2 or more lines of systemic therapy, including DLBCL not otherwise specified, primary mediastinal large B-cell lymphoma, high-grade B-cell lymphoma, and DLBCL arising from follicular lymphoma. Neither treatment indicates patients with primary central nervous system(CNS) lymphoma. According to the US National Institute of Health clinical trial registration, multiple clinical trials are underway with CAR-T cell treatment for different malignancies, including multiple myeloma, CNS tumors, hepatocellular carcinoma, lung cancer, etc. [3] [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: CART Cell Therapy Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clusters of differentiation-19 (CD-19), a B cell surface marker, -directed CAR-T cells show encouraging outcomes in a variety of malignancies, including pediatric and B-cell acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL), and chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). The first approved CAR-T therapy targeting CD-19, tisagenlecleucel (tisa-cel, ELIANA), was initially approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for patients with ALL in 2017, and later diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), high-grade B-cell lymphoma, and DLBCL arising from follicular lymphoma (JULIET). [6] [7] [8] Around the same time, axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel, ZUMA-1) gained approval for relapsed/refractory (R/R) DLBCL and later for R/R follicular lymphoma. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Follicular Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: CAR-T therapy is an option for patients with multiply relapsed follicular lymphomas. CAR-T cells are created from the patient's T-lymphocytes that have been genetically modified (transfected) with a gene encoding a CAR to direct the T-cells against the lymphoma cells. The T-cells are genetically changed ex vivo, multiplied in a production facility, and then reinfused into the patient as a form of treatment. Axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel) and tisagenlecleucel (tisa-cel) are CD19-directed CAR-T therapies that are approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). [30] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: CART Cell Therapy Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Since then, more agents such as lisocabtagene maraleucel (liso-cel) have made their way onto the market, demonstrating better tolerance. [11] Later, brexucabtagene autoleucel was approved for R/R mantle cell lymphoma (MCL). [12] More recently, anti-B-cell Maturation Antigen (BCMA) CAR-T, idecabtagene vicleucel (ide-cel, KarMMa), and ciltacabtagene autoleucel (cilta-cel, CARTITUDE-1) have been approved for R/R multiple myeloma. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Rituximab -- Administration -- Adult Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: The following are adult dosing regimens for approved indications. Clinicians should refer to institutional protocols and the packaging insert for indications, premedications, and dosing. Relapsed or refractory CD20-positive B-cell NHL: Monotherapy: 375 mg/m 2 /dose IV weekly for 4 or 8 weeks. Ibritumomab tiuxetan combination therapy: 250 mg/m 2 /dose IV x 1 on the first day, then on days 7, 8, or 9 of the regimen. Previously untreated CD20-positive B-cell NHL: Follicular type - combination chemotherapy: 375 mg/m 2 /dose IV x 1 on day 1 of each cycle for up to 8 cycles. Follicular type - ibritumomab tiuxetan combination therapy: 250 mg/m 2 /dose IV x 1 on the first day, then on days 7, 8, or 9 of the regimen. Diffuse large B-cell type - combination chemotherapy: 375 mg/m 2 /dose IV x 1 on day 1 of each cycle for up to 8 cycles. Non-progressing CD20-positive B-cell NHL: 375 mg/m 2 /dose IV x 1 on days 1, 8, 15, 22, or a 6-month cycle. CD-20-positive CLL/SLL:

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Mechanism of Action -- Several Immunotherapy Modalities Used in Cancer Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical Applications: CAR T-cell therapy has shown remarkable success in treating certain hematological malignancies, such as B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL) and certain types of non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL). It is also being investigated for solid tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Indications -- CAR T-cell therapy
retrieved_docs.content: B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (B-ALL): CAR T-cell therapy has demonstrated remarkable efficacy in treating relapsed or refractory B-ALL, particularly in pediatric and young adult patients. Diffuse Large B-cell Lymphoma (DLBCL): CAR T-cell therapy has been approved for certain subtypes of DLBCL, including relapsed or refractory DLBCL after 2 or more lines of systemic therapy. Follicular Lymphoma (FL): CAR T-cell therapy has demonstrated promising results in treating relapsed or refractory FL, especially in patients who have failed multiple lines of therapy. Mantle Cell Lymphoma (MCL): CAR T-cell therapy has shown efficacy in treating relapsed or refractory MCL, particularly in patients who have failed prior therapies. Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL): While still investigational, CAR T-cell therapy is being studied in CLL, particularly in patients with high-risk disease or those who have relapsed after multiple lines of therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: T-Cell Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment regimens now under phase III are  Brentuximab Vedotin (anti CD30 monoclonal antibody) [10] and CHP vs. CHOP,  Romidepsin (HDAC inhibitor) CHOP (Ro-CHOP) Versus CHOP, these two for induction and Pralatrexate Versus Observation Following CHOP-based Chemotherapy for maintenance. In ALCL, CD 30 expression is assessed; if positive, the treatment regimen brentuximab vedotin, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and prednisone (BV +CHP), is considered superior to CHOP.ALK positivity in ALCL also plays an important role as ALK inhibitors(Crizotinib, Alectinib). Extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma is generally refractory to CHOP regimens because of the presence of the P-glycoprotein gene. For localized variants, chemoradiotherapy using dexamethasone, carboplatin, ifosfamide, and etoposide, whereas for advanced disease, L-asparaginase with ifosfamide, etoposide, dexamethasone, and methotrexate (SMILE). Adult T-cell lymphoma-leukemia has a very poor prognosis. Because of the rapid progression of the disease, the most appropriate treatment modality is unclear, and the patient should be enrolled in a clinical trial. The current treatment modality is VCAP (vincristine, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and prednisone), AMP (doxorubicin, ranimustine, and prednisone), VECP (vindesine, etoposide, carboplatin, prednisone), and intrathecal chemotherapy with the addition of anti-CCR4 antibody mogamulizumab. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Peripheral T-Cell Lymphoma  -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: There is no cure for AITL outside the realm of bone marrow transplantation. Early involvement of a hematologist specializing in malignant hematology to choose an ideal chemotherapy regimen to induce remission (either complete or partial remission) in the “transplant eligible” patient is important. In patients who are “transplant-ineligible,” the strategy should be to choose an ideal regimen to increase progression-free survival. A dermatologist, radiation oncologist, and hematologist should evaluate patients with cutaneous T cell lymphomas (MF and PCALCL) to choose an ideal therapeutic regimen that involves a combination of skin directed therapy and systemic therapy or one of them.

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Follicular Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Immunotherapy or chemoimmunotherapy alone is not likely to result in long-term survival for patients who experience early treatment failure. Patients who have a complete response to chemoimmunotherapy [bendamustine plus obinutuzumab (BG) or obinutuzumab plus CHOP (G-CHOP)] may benefit from autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) as it improves survival. Individuals who have not achieved a complete response may be good candidates for chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) treatment or 1 of the novel agents discussed below. For patients who are not HSCT candidates, using novel agents such as lenalidomide, copanlisib, and tazemetostat is recommended. Several retrospective analyses suggest autologous HSCT improves survival when compared with chemoimmunotherapy alone. [27] [28] [29] High-dose chemotherapies with autologous HSCT (hematologic rescue) and nonmyeloablative or reduced-intensity allogeneic HSCT have been used to treat follicular lymphoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Rituximab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: CD20-positive B-cell non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma (NHL) Approved for use as a single agent in relapsed/refractory, low-grade, or follicular B cell NHL Single-agent maintenance therapy for patients with previously untreated follicular B cell NHL who are achieving a complete or some degree of response to combination chemotherapy with rituximab As a single agent in low-grade non-progressive/stable B cell NHL after Cyclophosphamide, Vincristine, and Prednisolone (CVP) chemotherapy. In combination with anthracycline-based chemotherapy in previously untreated diffuse large B cell NHL Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) or small lymphocytic leukemia (SLL): Approved for use in combination with fludarabine and cyclophosphamide to treat newly diagnosed or previously treated CLL.

retrieved_docs.content: Besides the 2 approved medications targeting CD19 on B lymphocytic cells treating B-cell malignancies, multiple tumor-associated antigens have been under investigation in targeting various types of cancer, especially in solid tumors. retrieved_docs.title: Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for CART therapy. Determine the complications of CART therapy. Assess the contraindications of CART therapy. Communicate some interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination in patients who receive CART therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Follicular Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Tisa-cel – Among the 90 patients with follicular lymphoma treated with tisa-cel, the ORR was 86 percent (69 percent CR, 17 percent PR) with a median time to first response of 2.9 months. The median duration of response was not reached, with a median follow-up of 9.1 months. The estimated PFS at 12 months was 71 percent (95% CI 58-80 percent). CRS of any grade was reported in 53 percent, all less than grade 3. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Mantle Cell Lymphoma -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Chemoimmunotherapy is conventionally the choice as front-line therapy among patients necessitating treatment with a potential role of targeted therapies in the salvage setting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 765
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are a class of targeted therapies that block the BTK enzyme, a key component of B‑cell receptor signaling. Ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are both members of this BTK inhibitor class, sharing the same mechanism of action. Ibrutinib may treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia) by inhibiting malignant B‑cell proliferation. Recognizing that both ibrutinib and zanubrutinib belong to the BTK inhibitor family informs clinicians when selecting appropriate targeted agents for patients with B‑cell malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Gilteritinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), offering a potential option for patients with this aggressive hematologic malignancy.

The systematic categorization of these agents under the GDC property of Pharmacologic Substance underscores their clinical relevance for decision‑making and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: are also linked to the antitumor category; human herpesvirus 1 functions as a vector in oncolytic virotherapy and talimogene laherparepvec, an engineered oncolytic herpesvirus approved for melanoma, is contraindicated in patients with leukemia, underscoring specific safety considerations. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that antitumor agents span traditional cytotoxic drugs, corticosteroids, targeted inhibitors, and emerging viral therapies, reflecting the multifaceted strategies employed in contemporary cancer treatment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 328
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are a class of protein kinase inhibitors that also function as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Both ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members of this BTK inhibitor class. Ibrutinib, as a BTK inhibitor, may be used in the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن).

Collectively, these agents—ranging from traditional chemotherapeutics and central‑nervous‑system depressants to modern immunotherapies—illustrate the comprehensive pharmacologic approach to Hodgkin disease, emphasizing individualized regimen selection to optimize patient outcomes and underscoring the importance of systematic drug classification for clinical research and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 723
retrieved_docs.content: and chlormethin hydrochlorid further contribute to the chemotherapeutic arsenal. Targeted and immunomodulatory therapies are highlighted by brentuximab vedotin (a monoclonal antibody‑drug conjugate), pembrolizumab (a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor) and sargramostim (a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor), each directing immune mechanisms against Hodgkin lymphoma cells. Collectively, these relationships illustrate a multimodal treatment paradigm that integrates alkylators, platinum compounds, anthracyclines, antimitotics, antibiotics, monoclonal antibodies and immune‑stimulating agents—used alone or in combination—to manage Hodgkin disease, underscoring the importance of diverse chemotherapy regimens and emerging immunotherapies in contemporary patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 83
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Within this subclass, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are recognized members, and ibrutinib has therapeutic activity against chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Another grouping, designated as other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC, also belongs to the broader class of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin are members of this ATC group, and both gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Together, these kinase‑targeting drugs illustrate the role of protein kinase inhibition in the management of hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia.
